entry.title: [BIP-0054] 64-Byte Transactions and Potential Legitimate Uses
entry.summary:
In a recent discussion on the Bitcoin Development Mailing List, Chris Stewart acknowledged Jeremy's concerns about BIP 54, particularly regarding its clarity on witness-stripped serialized size. Stewart agreed that the proposal could benefit from clearer language and committed to updating it. This change is intended to address potential ambiguities in how the policy handles transactions of specific sizes.
Jeremy raised an important point about the implications of invalidating 64-byte transactions within the context of advanced Bitcoin scripting features that might be introduced in the future. He argued that such a limitation could restrict the flexibility of script development, suggesting that this issue warrants more attention in the rationale section of BIP 54. However, the counterargument presented in the discussion highlights that, under the current model where coins commit to their spending conditions, a 64-byte transaction typically results in either the burning of the coin's value or allowing anyone to spend it. This scenario persists regardless of any sophisticated introspective or covenant features integrated into the spending conditions.
The dialogue also touched on the feasibility of designing a plausible Bitcoin scripting upgrade that could accommodate a 64-byte witness-stripped size exception while being realistic and useful under future Bitcoin protocols. The consensus appears to lean towards the improbability of such a transaction structure becoming relevant or necessary, given the fundamental design and security principles of Bitcoin. This perspective underscores the challenges in balancing forward-looking script innovation with the technical realities and security imperatives of the cryptocurrency's architecture.
